---
title: "The beta-binomial pathology screen in `.load_screened()`"
author: "Daniel Noble"
date: 2026-06-11
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
    theme: cosmo
    code-fold: show
    embed-resources: true
editor_options:
  chunk_output_type: console
---

## The question

In `.load_screened()` (defined inline in `ms/ms.qmd` and `ms/supplement.qmd`)
the simulation-results figures screen the two-stage beta-binomial estimator:

```r
bad <- ps |>
  dplyr::filter(method == "two_stage_bb",
                (quantity == "z"         & abs(bias) > 20) |
                (quantity == "CTmax_1hr" & abs(bias) > 10)) |>
  dplyr::distinct(scenario, sim_id) |>
  dplyr::mutate(bb_path = TRUE)
```

Two questions: (1) is this flagging runs with extreme bias on the assumption
they had convergence problems? (2) why does the `two_stage_bb` slice look like
~4000 rows when there are only 1000 simulations?

## What the screen does

`ps` is the bound stack of `per_sim_<prefix>_*.rds` files. Each per-sim file is
**1000 sims × 12 rows** = 6 methods (`joint_4pl`, `joint_4pl_abs`,
`two_stage_bin`, `two_stage_bin_t`, `two_stage_bb`, `two_stage_bb_t`) × 2
quantities (`z`, `CTmax_1hr`).

**Yes — it is a convergence proxy.** The true values are z ≈ 6.7 °C and
CTmax ≈ 32 °C, so `|z bias| > 20` or `|CTmax bias| > 10` is far outside any
plausible estimate. A bias that large is not a noisy estimate, it is a
**degenerate / non-converged `glmmTMB` beta-binomial Stage-1 fit** — one
temperature's LT50 has blown up and tilted the Stage-2 line. The thresholds are
deliberately loose so the screen catches only catastrophic failures, not
ordinary variability.

**It is charitable to the two-stage.** Removing the worst beta-binomial blow-ups
*before* computing bias/coverage makes `two_stage_bb` look **better and more
comparable** than if every run were kept — a conservative choice so the classical
pipeline is not strawmanned. The screen drops only the BB rows
(`two_stage_bb`, `two_stage_bb_t`) for a flagged sim via the later
`left_join(bad) |> filter(is.na(bb_path) | !(method %in% c("two_stage_bb",
"two_stage_bb_t")))`; the `joint_4pl` and `two_stage_bin` rows for the same sim
are retained.

## The "4000" vs the size of `bad`

These are two different objects:

- **The 4000 is the input the filter scans** — the number of `two_stage_bb` rows
  in `ps`: for a `scenN` prefix that matches the n3 and n5 files,
  `2 files × 2 quantities (z, CTmax) × 1000 sims = 4000`. That decomposition
  (scenario × quantity × sims) is exactly right *for this count*.
- **`bad` is the output, and it is tiny.** After
  `filter(abs(bias) > threshold) |> distinct(scenario, sim_id)`, `bad` holds
  **one row per genuinely pathological simulation**. On the completed cells that
  is 5 (scen1), 2 (scen2), 0 (scen3) — beta-binomial blow-ups are rare.
- **`distinct(scenario, sim_id)` drops the quantity dimension.** `bad` is keyed
  only by `(scenario, sim_id)`, so a sim flagged on z *or* CTmax appears once;
  the "× z and CTmax" doubling does **not** survive into `bad`. The scenario
  (n3/n5) dimension *does* survive, because it is part of the key.

Measured on the completed cells:

| prefix | files | `two_stage_bb` rows | pathological (pre-`distinct`) | `bad` (post-`distinct`) |
|--------|-------|--------------------:|------------------------------:|------------------------:|
| scen1  | 2     | 4000                | 9                             | 5                       |
| scen2  | 2     | 4000                | 3                             | 2                       |
| scen3  | 2     | 4000                | 0                             | 0                       |

So the 4000 is the full `two_stage_bb` slice (every sim × both quantities × both
replication levels); `bad` is the small subset that trips the extreme-bias cutoff,
de-duplicated to one entry per simulation.

## Caveat to remember

This screen is an **analyst degree of freedom** that favours the two-stage. It
should be reported transparently (it is, in the figure captions / methods), and
the pathology counts above show it removes only a handful of runs — so it does
not materially flatter the two-stage, but the direction of the choice (charitable
to the classical pipeline) is the conservative one.
